topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The mail slot clattered, a cheap, unwelcome announcement in the quiet afternoon. I pulled the thick envelope out, my fingers brushing against the unfamiliar texture of the paper. Inside, a card. Unsigned. My stomach tightened with a low, irritated rumble. Another piece of unsolicited correspondence cluttering my inbox. I unfolded it slowly, my gaze sweeping over the hastily scrawled, overly cheerful lettering. It was saccharine, the kind of forced enthusiasm that grated on my nerves. I folded it with unnecessary precision, the crisp edges of the paper feeling sharp under my touch. I tucked it into a drawer, a silent dismissal already settling over me. Why did I even bother looking?

[story 2]

I stared at the card as if it were a particularly offensive stain on the pavement. It sat there, bright colors mocking the muted tones of my living room. There was no name, just a generic sentiment about 'a wonderful day.' A slow, tight smile pulled at the corners of my mouth, quickly suppressed. I picked it up, turning it over once, examining the amateurish handwriting. It felt like an imposition, an unnecessary intrusion into my carefully curated solitude. I dropped it back into the box with a soft thud, the sound echoing the feeling of wanting to erase whatever it was. I needed to focus on things that actually held value, not these flimsy tokens of forced goodwill.

[story 3]

The envelope felt flimsy, cheap cardboard against my palm. When I finally cracked it open, the contents were immediately irritating: a card, utterly devoid of personalization. I let out a small, sharp exhale through my nose. This was just more noise. I scanned the message—a generic birthday wish—and a distinct stiffness entered my shoulders. I didn't even bother reading the sentiment fully; I just registered its existence as an inconvenience. With a flick of my wrist, I slid the card back into the pile, not bothering to address it further. It was pointless, an artifact of someone else's misplaced effort, and I had no intention of acknowledging it.
